content:
# Disagreement Log — Leviticus 4

**Chapter:** Leviticus 4 — The Sin Offering (Chattat)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Date processed:** 2026-03-05
**Total disagreements:** 31
**Major rulings issued:** 7

---

## Summary of Major Rulings

| # | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | Ruling |
|---|-------|------|-------|--------|--------|
| MR-1 | chattat: "sin offering" vs. "purification offering" | sin offering | sin offering | purification offering | **"sin offering"** — with footnote acknowledging purificatory function |
| MR-2 | Structure of vv.1–2 (introductory formula) | hanging protasis with dash | conditional "If any person" | collapsed prose | **LOGOS** structure adopted |
| MR-3 | 'edah: "congregation" vs. "community" | congregation | congregation | community | **"congregation"** |
| MR-4 | nasi: "leader" vs. "ruler" | leader | leader | leader | **"leader"** — unanimous, confirmed |
| MR-5 | shelamim: "peace offerings" vs. "fellowship sacrifice" | peace offerings | peace offerings | fellowship sacrifice | **"peace offerings"** — series consistency |
| MR-6 | Verse-opening "And" convention | "And" throughout | "And" (most) | drops "And" frequently | **Retain "And"** in procedural sequences; natural English at case pivots |
| MR-7 | tamim: "without blemish" vs. "without defect" | without blemish | without blemish | without defect | **"without blemish"** |

---

## Verse-by-Verse Disagreement Log

### v.1

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Opening verb | "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" | "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying," | "The LORD spoke to Moses:" |
| Punctuation | Colon after "saying" | Comma after "saying" | Colon only after "Moses" |

**Disagreements:** 2
**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" adopted. PNEUMA drops the vav-consecutive "And," contrary to series convention. Comma vs. colon: colon adopted (EMET) since the speech follows immediately.

---

### v.2

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Opening | "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: 'A person [nefesh] when he sins unintentionally [bi-shgagah]..." | "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: If any person sins unintentionally in any of the things the LORD has commanded not to be done..." | "Speak to the Israelites and say: When a person sins unintentionally — by doing something the LORD has commanded must not be done — the following applies." |
| Address formula | "sons of Israel" | "sons of Israel" | "Israelites" |
| Conditional structure | Hanging protasis (dash at end) | Clean conditional: "If any person" | Resolved clause: "the following applies" |
| "nefesh" preserved? | Yes — bracketed | No | No |
| nefesh rendered | "[nefesh]" gloss | "any person" | "a person" |
| bi-shgagah phrase | "against any of the commandments of the LORD concerning things that are not to be done" | "in any of the things the LORD has commanded not to be done" | "by doing something the LORD has commanded must not be done" |

**Disagreements:** 6
**Rulings:**
- "Sons of Israel" (EMET/LOGOS) over "Israelites" (PNEUMA) — series convention.
- LOGOS's conditional structure adopted (MR-2).
- Nefesh gloss not retained in body text; addressed in textual notes.
- "in any of the things the LORD has commanded not to be done" (LOGOS) over EMET's longer phrase and PNEUMA's "something."

---

### v.3

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| chattat | "sin offering" | "sin offering" | "purification offering" |
| Opening clause | "if the anointed priest [kohen ha-mashiach] sins, bringing guilt [le-ashmah] upon the people, then he shall bring near..." | "if it is the anointed priest who sins, thus bringing guilt on the people — then he shall bring to the LORD for the sin that he has committed..." | "If it is the anointed priest who has sinned, and his sin brings guilt on the people, he shall bring to the LORD, as his purification offering for the sin he has committed..." |
| "bull" specified as | "bull [par] of the herd" | "bull from the herd" | "unblemished young bull" (merges tamim) |
| tamim placement | "without blemish" separate clause | "without blemish" | merged into "unblemished" |
| Verb: "bring near" | "shall bring near" (hiqrib) | "shall bring to the LORD" | "shall bring to the LORD" |

**Disagreements:** 5
**Rulings:**
- chattat ruling: MR-1. "sin offering" with footnote.
- LOGOS's structure adopted: "if it is the anointed priest who has sinned, bringing guilt upon the people..."
- "Without blemish" kept as separate clause (MR-7); "unblemished" rejected.
- "Bring to the LORD" adopted over EMET's "bring near" — hiqrib means "to bring near / to present as offering" but "bring to the LORD" captures both the movement and the dedication without requiring a gloss.

---

### v.4

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Opening "And" | "And he shall bring..." | "He shall bring..." | "He shall bring..." |
| Verb repetition | "he shall lay his hand...and he shall slaughter the bull" | "he shall lay his hand...and slaughter the bull" | "He shall lay his hand...and slaughter it" |

**Disagreements:** 2
**Rulings:**
- "And" retained (MR-6).
- "Slaughter the bull" (EMET/LOGOS) over PNEUMA's "slaughter it" — the explicit noun is repeated in the Hebrew (yishchat et-ha-par) at first mention.

---

### v.5

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Opening | "And the anointed priest shall take..." | "Then the anointed priest shall take..." | "The anointed priest shall then take..." |
| Blood phrase | "some of the blood of the bull" | "some of the blood of the bull" | "some of the bull's blood" |

**Disagreements:** 2
**Ruling:** "Then the anointed priest" (LOGOS) adopted — the Hebrew ve-laqach introduces a new action step. "Some of the blood of the bull" (EMET/LOGOS) preferred as it specifies the source; "bull's blood" is natural English but the genitive ordering in the Hebrew (dam ha-par) is more naturally rendered in the expanded form.

---

### v.6

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| "parokhet" rendered | "veil [parokhet] of the sanctuary" | "veil of the sanctuary" | "veil of the sanctuary" |
| Hebrew gloss | Yes, bracketed | No | No |

**Disagreements:** 1
**Ruling:** No bracketed gloss in the body text. "Veil of the sanctuary" — all three agree on this rendering; confirmed.

---

### v.7

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Incense altar name | "altar of aromatic incense" | "altar of fragrant incense" | "altar of fragrant incense" |
| "All the remaining blood" phrase | "all the remaining blood of the bull" | "all the rest of the blood of the bull" | "all the remaining blood" |

**Disagreements:** 2
**Rulings:**
- "Altar of fragrant incense" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) over EMET's "aromatic." "Fragrant" is more precise; qetoret (incense) is specifically a sweet/fragrant offering.
- "All the remaining blood of the bull" (EMET) over LOGOS's "all the rest of." The Hebrew kol-dam ha-par ("all the blood of the bull") with the context of "remaining" is better preserved as "all the remaining blood of the bull."

---

### v.8

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Fat phrase | "the fat that covers the entrails [ha-mekhasseh al-ha-qerev] and all the fat that is upon the entrails" | "the fat covering the entrails and all the fat that is on the entrails" | "the fat that covers the inner organs, all the fat that clings to them" |
| "entrails" vs. "inner organs" | entrails | entrails | inner organs |

**Disagreements:** 2
**Rulings:**
- "Entrails" (EMET/LOGOS) over PNEUMA's "inner organs." "Entrails" is more specific and carries the anatomical weight of the Hebrew qerev. "Inner organs" is a vague paraphrase.
- "The fat that covers the entrails" (LOGOS — more natural English syntax) over EMET's more wooden order.

---

### v.9

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Liver portion | "the appendage [ha-yoteret] upon the liver — upon the kidneys he shall remove it" | "the lobe of the liver, which he shall remove with the kidneys" | "the lobe of the liver, which he shall remove with the kidneys" |
| yoteret rendered | "appendage" | "lobe" | "lobe" |
| "flanks" vs. "loins" | "flanks [al-ha-kesalim]" | "loins" | "loins" |

**Disagreements:** 3
**Rulings:**
- "Lobe of the liver" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) over EMET's "appendage." The yoteret is the caudate lobe or the fatty lobe attached to the liver; "lobe" is more anatomically recognizable.
- "Loins" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) over EMET's "flanks." The Hebrew kesalim refers to the flank/loin area. Both are defensible; "loins" is used in LXX (ὀσφύν) and is more recognizable.
- "Which he shall remove with the kidneys" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — the Hebrew construction "upon the kidneys he shall remove it" (EMET) is awkwardly literal. The consensus adopts the cleaner LOGOS/PNEUMA phrasing.

---

### v.10

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| "lifted off" vs. "taken" | "just as it is lifted off from the ox" | "just as it is taken from the ox" | "just as it is removed from the ox" |
| shelamim | "sacrifice of peace offerings" | "sacrifice of peace offerings" | "fellowship sacrifice" |

**Disagreements:** 2
**Rulings:**
- "Taken" (LOGOS) adopted over EMET's "lifted off" and PNEUMA's "removed." The Hebrew herim is "to lift up / take up" — all three capture this. "Taken" is the most natural English here.
- "Sacrifice of peace offerings" (EMET/LOGOS) — MR-5 confirmed.

---

### v.11

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Opening | "And the hide of the bull..." | "But the skin of the bull..." | "But the bull's hide..." |
| "hide" vs. "skin" | hide | skin | hide |

**Disagreements:** 2
**Rulings:**
- "Hide" (EMET/PNEUMA) over LOGOS's "skin." The Hebrew 'or refers specifically to the animal skin-as-hide; "hide" carries the appropriate livestock register.
- "And" (EMET) retained over "But." The Hebrew vav here is a contrastive connector (indicating what is NOT burned on the altar), which "But" captures (LOGOS/PNEUMA). However, in the series the vav-consecutive rendering is "And" throughout the procedural sequences. Given the genuine contrast here, the consensus adopts "But" as it aids comprehension.

*Revised ruling: "But the hide" adopted — the contrast with vv.8-10 is structural, and "But" serves the reader better than "And."*

---

### v.12

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| "ash heap" | "ash heap [el-shefekh ha-deshen]" | "the place where the ashes are poured out" | "the ash heap" |
| "clean place" | "clean place [maqom tahor]" | "clean place" | "ceremonially clean place" |

**Disagreements:** 2
**Rulings:**
- "Ash heap" (EMET/PNEUMA) over LOGOS's descriptive paraphrase. LOGOS accurately unpacks the root (shafakh = to pour out) but the paraphrase is long; "ash heap" is the standard English equivalent for shefekh ha-deshen.
- "Clean place" (EMET/LOGOS) over PNEUMA's "ceremonially clean place." The Hebrew maqom tahor is simply "clean place" — tahor denotes ritual/cultic cleanliness, but adding "ceremonially" front-loads an interpretive gloss. The established series practice has been to translate tahor as "clean" and handle the cultic register in notes.

---

### v.13

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| 'edah | "congregation" | "congregation" | "community" |
| "hidden from the eyes" | "hidden from the eyes of the assembly" | "hidden from the eyes of the assembly" | "escapes the notice of the assembly" |
| qahal | "assembly" | "assembly" | "assembly" |
| Guilt phrase | "and they incur guilt [ve-asheemu]" | "and they bear guilt" | "and bear guilt" |

**Disagreements:** 3
**Rulings:**
- "Congregation" confirmed (MR-3).
- "Hidden from the eyes of the assembly" (EMET/LOGOS) over PNEUMA's "escapes the notice." The Hebrew ne'elam me-einei ha-qahal is literally "was hidden from the eyes of the assembly." The concrete imagery ("eyes of the assembly") is deliberately preserved in the Hebrew and should be in the translation.
- "Incur guilt" (EMET) preferred over "bear guilt" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Hebrew ve-asheemu (qal perfect of 'asham, "to incur guilt / become guilty") has an inceptive force: they become guilty, they take on guilt. "Incur" captures this better than "bear" (which suggests guilt already held).

---

### v.14

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Opening clause | "when the sin that they sinned against it becomes known" | "when the sin which they have committed becomes known" | "then, when the sin they have committed becomes known" |
| "sinned against it" | present | absent | absent |
| "assembly shall bring near" | "shall bring near" | "shall offer" | "shall bring" |

**Disagreements:** 3
**Rulings:**
- EMET's "sinned against it" (the Hebrew vehet'u aleha — "they sinned against it") is awkward as a relative clause. LOGOS/PNEUMA smooth it; consensus adopts "when the sin they have committed becomes known."
- "Shall bring" (PNEUMA) over LOGOS's "shall offer" and EMET's "shall bring near." The Hebrew ve-hiqrivu means "shall bring near / present" — both EMET and the original convey the cultic presentation. "Bring" without qualification is slightly flat; "bring near" is too literal. The consensus adopts "shall bring ... and present it before the tent of meeting" to capture both verbs in v.14.

---

### v.15

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Subject of slaughter | "he shall slaughter the bull" (ambiguous subject) | "the bull shall be slaughtered" (passive) | "the bull shall be slaughtered" (passive) |

**Disagreements:** 1
**Ruling:** Passive "shall be slaughtered" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) adopted — see Council Notes above.

---

### v.17

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Blood phrase | "some of the blood" | "some of the blood" | "the blood" |
| "before the veil" | "before the veil" | "before the veil" | "in front of the veil" |

**Disagreements:** 2
**Rulings:**
- "Some of the blood" (EMET/LOGOS) retained over PNEUMA's "the blood" — the Hebrew mi-dam (partitive, "from the blood") must be preserved.
- "Before the veil" (EMET/LOGOS) over "in front of" — a minor stylistic preference; both are accurate. "Before" carries a slight connotation of presentational direction (the priest presents the blood before the veil/before the LORD), which is theologically appropriate.

---

### v.20

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Formula phrase | "just as he did with the bull of the sin offering; thus shall he do with it" | "just as he did with the bull of the sin offering; so shall he do with it" | "exactly as he did with the bull for the priest's own purification offering" |
| chattat term | "sin offering" | "sin offering" | "purification offering" |
| Forgiveness passive | "it shall be forgiven them" | "they shall be forgiven" | "they will be forgiven" |
| Tense of forgiveness | shall | shall | will |

**Disagreements:** 4
**Rulings:**
- "Thus shall he do with it" (EMET) adopted — the Hebrew ken ya'aseh lo is a formal legal echo phrase that should be preserved verbatim.
- chattat: MR-1.
- "They shall be forgiven" — the Hebrew ve-nislach lahem is literally "and it shall be forgiven to them." The subject is the forgiveness (or God is implied as subject). "They shall be forgiven" (LOGOS) is the most natural English passive. PNEUMA's "will be forgiven" (future indicative) is also accurate but loses the modal-legal register of "shall" appropriate to Levitical legislation.
- "It shall be forgiven them" (EMET) vs. "they shall be forgiven" (LOGOS): consensus adopts LOGOS — more natural English, same theological content.

---

### v.22

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Case introduction | "When a leader [nasi] sins and does unintentionally any of the commandments of the LORD his God concerning things that are not to be done, and he incurs guilt —" | "When a leader sins, doing unintentionally any one of all the things the LORD his God has commanded not to be done, and he becomes guilty —" | "When a leader sins unintentionally and does something forbidden by any of the commands of the LORD his God —" |
| "commandments" vs. "things" | commandments | things | commands |
| "incurs guilt" vs. "becomes guilty" | incurs guilt | becomes guilty | (omitted, absorbed into v.23) |

**Disagreements:** 3
**Rulings:**
- "Things the LORD his God has commanded not to be done" (LOGOS) adopted over EMET's "commandments...concerning things" (slightly redundant) and PNEUMA's "something forbidden" (too loose).
- "Incurs guilt" (EMET) over "becomes guilty" (LOGOS) — see v.13 ruling; 'asham has an inceptive force better captured by "incurs."
- PNEUMA's omission of the guilt clause is a substantive error; the Hebrew ve-ashem ("and he incurs guilt") is a distinct legal element. Retained.

---

### v.23

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| "sin...made known" | "his sin that he sinned is made known to him" | "his sin which he has committed is made known to him" | "when he is made aware of his sin" |
| Animal phrase | "a male goat [se'ir izzim zachar]" | "a goat, a male without blemish" | "a male goat without defect" |
| "without blemish" | separate clause at end | after "male" | "without defect" |

**Disagreements:** 3
**Rulings:**
- "His sin that he has committed is made known to him" — combining EMET's relative pronoun structure and LOGOS's "committed" phrasing.
- "A male goat without blemish" (combining LOGOS structure with EMET's "without blemish" — MR-7 confirmed).
- "Without defect" rejected (MR-7).

---

### v.24

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Slaughter location | "in the place [ba-maqom] where he slaughters the burnt offering" | "in the place where they slaughter the burnt offering" | "in the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered" |
| Subject of slaughter | he (singular) | they (generic plural) | passive |

**Disagreements:** 1 (substantive: subject of slaughter)
**Ruling:** PNEUMA's passive "is slaughtered" adopted as most natural English and theologically neutral. The Hebrew yishchat is third-person singular but contextually impersonal here; all three renderings are defensible.

---

### v.26

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Fat phrase | "like the fat of the sacrifice of peace offerings" | "as the fat of the sacrifice of peace offerings" | "just as the fat of the fellowship sacrifice is burned" |
| shelamim | "peace offerings" | "peace offerings" | "fellowship sacrifice" |

**Disagreements:** 2
**Rulings:**
- "Like the fat of the sacrifice of peace offerings" (EMET) — "like" (ka-chelev) is closer to the Hebrew comparative; LOGOS's "as" is equivalent. Consensus: "as" (slightly more natural).
- shelamim: MR-5 confirmed.

---

### v.27

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| am ha-aretz | "people of the land [am ha-aretz]" | "common people" | "member of the community" |
| Subject number | "any person...he" | "any one...he" | "any member...they" (gender-inclusive) |

**Disagreements:** 3
**Rulings:**
- "Common people" — see Council Notes; adopted over EMET's "people of the land" (risks post-exilic connotations) and PNEUMA's "member of the community" (loses the am ha-aretz distinctiveness).
- "Any person" (EMET, singular) over PNEUMA's "any member...they" (gender-inclusive plural). The Hebrew nefesh (v.27, implied) and 'asher (v.28) are grammatically singular. The consensus maintains the Hebrew's singular structure in the legal formulation; gender-inclusive readings should not alter grammatical number in legislative texts.

---

### v.28

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Female goat | "a female goat [se'irat izzim]" | "a goat, a female without blemish" | "a female goat without defect" |
| "without blemish" position | "without blemish" at end | "a female without blemish" | "without defect" |

**Disagreements:** 2
**Rulings:**
- "A female goat without blemish" — combining all three elements (species, gender, quality).
- "Without blemish" (MR-7).

---

### v.29

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| Subject: who lays hand | "And he shall lay his hand" | "And he shall lay his hand" | "They shall lay their hand" |

**Disagreements:** 1
**Ruling:** "He shall lay his hand" (EMET/LOGOS) — the Hebrew singular is maintained. See v.27 ruling. PNEUMA's plural is a gender-inclusive adjustment not warranted by the Hebrew.

---

### v.31

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| re'ach nichoach | "a pleasing aroma [re'ach nichoach] to the LORD" | "for a pleasing aroma to the LORD" | "as a fragrant aroma to the LORD" |
| "pleasing" vs. "fragrant" | pleasing | pleasing | fragrant |

**Disagreements:** 2
**Rulings:**
- "For a pleasing aroma to the LORD" (LOGOS) — the "for" (le-) prepositional phrase is correct; EMET's "as" also works. "For" is slightly more precise as to purpose.
- "Pleasing" (EMET/LOGOS) over PNEUMA's "fragrant" — MR-7 parallel; "pleasing" renders the theological nichoach (rest / pleasure) content; "fragrant" only renders the sensory re'ach.

---

### v.32

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| "his offering for a sin offering" | "as his offering for a sin offering" | "as his offering for a sin offering" | "as their purification offering" |
| chattat | "sin offering" | "sin offering" | "purification offering" |

**Disagreements:** 1 (plus chattat — MR-1)
**Ruling:** "As his offering for a sin offering" (EMET/LOGOS) — slightly redundant but preserves the Hebrew 'et-qorbano le-chattat. chattat: MR-1.

---

### v.33

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| "slaughter it as a sin offering" | "slaughter it as a sin offering in the place where he slaughters the burnt offering" | "slaughter it for a sin offering in the place where they slaughter the burnt offering" | "slaughter it at the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered" |

**Disagreements:** 1
**Ruling:** "Slaughter it as a sin offering in the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered" — combining EMET's "as a sin offering" (the Hebrew le-chattat, for/as a sin offering) with PNEUMA's passive construction for the location phrase.

---

### v.35

| Field | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|
| ishei YHWH | "upon the fire offerings [ishei] of the LORD" | "on the fire offerings of the LORD" | "on top of the fire-offerings to the LORD" |
| "for his sin that he sinned" | "for his sin that he sinned" | "for the sin which he has committed" | "for the sin they have committed" |

**Disagreements:** 3
**Rulings:**
- "Upon the fire offerings of the LORD" (EMET) adopted — "upon" (al) is the correct preposition; LOGOS's "on" is equivalent; PNEUMA's "on top of" is slightly awkward.
- "For his sin that he has committed" — combining EMET's "his sin" (singular, consistent with ruling at v.27) and LOGOS's "has committed" (perfect aspect, consistent with other case-closing formulas).
- PNEUMA's plural "they" rejected — see v.27 ruling.

---

## Disagreement Count Summary

| Verse | Disagreements |
|-------|--------------|
| v.1 | 2 |
| v.2 | 6 |
| v.3 | 5 |
| v.4 | 2 |
| v.5 | 2 |
| v.6 | 1 |
| v.7 | 2 |
| v.8 | 2 |
| v.9 | 3 |
| v.10 | 2 |
| v.11 | 2 |
| v.12 | 2 |
| v.13 | 3 |
| v.14 | 3 |
| v.15 | 1 |
| v.17 | 2 |
| v.20 | 4 |
| v.22 | 3 |
| v.23 | 3 |
| v.24 | 1 |
| v.26 | 2 |
| v.27 | 3 |
| v.28 | 2 |
| v.29 | 1 |
| v.31 | 2 |
| v.32 | 1 |
| v.33 | 1 |
| v.35 | 3 |
| vv.16,18,19,21,25,30,34 | 0 (full agreement) |
| **TOTAL** | **31** |

---

## Recurring Disagreement Patterns

1. **chattat terminology (MR-1):** PNEUMA systematically uses "purification offering" (13 occurrences in Lev 4); EMET/LOGOS use "sin offering." This single terminological choice accounts for the largest block of PNEUMA's divergence from the other two translators and from the LXX tradition.

2. **Gender-inclusive singular-to-plural shifts (PNEUMA):** PNEUMA repeatedly renders Hebrew singular legal subjects as English plural ("they") for gender-inclusive reasons. This occurs at vv.27-35 (the common-person case). While the pastoral motivation is understandable, the grammatical number of a legislative text is a text-critical datum, not merely a stylistic choice. SOPHIA rules against number-shifting in legal formulations.

3. **shelamim (MR-5):** PNEUMA's "fellowship sacrifice" is a defensible alternative but was ruled against on series consistency grounds. This pattern will recur wherever shelamim appears in Leviticus.

4. **"And" as verse-opener (MR-6):** PNEUMA regularly drops the vav-consecutive "And." This reflects a dynamic priority (readability) over a formal priority (syntactic fidelity). The consensus retains "And" in procedural sequences as an established series convention.

5. **Tamim: "without blemish" vs. "without defect" (MR-7):** PNEUMA uses "without defect" throughout. EMET/LOGOS use "without blemish." This is a series-level term established in Leviticus 1.

---

## Notes for Future Chapters

- **Leviticus 5** will introduce the graduated-poverty provisions for the chattat (pigeon, turtledove, fine flour). The chattat ruling (MR-1: "sin offering") must be applied consistently there.
- **Leviticus 6:24–30** contains the priestly legislation for consuming the sin offering meat (the cases where blood was not taken inside the tent). The two-tier distinction established in this chapter is crucial background.
- **Leviticus 16** (Yom Kippur) extends the blood-gradient logic into the Most Holy Place itself — the kapporet (mercy seat/atonement cover). The sevenfold sprinkling of Lev 4:6 finds its culmination in Lev 16's entry behind the veil. The series will need to maintain terminological consistency: kipper, parokhet, chattat, nislach.
- **Numbers 15:22–29** revisits the unintentional-sin category (bi-shgagah) and will require the same foundational vocabulary established here.
